questions.questionText: A butterfly-shaped erythematous rash over the malar eminences sparing the nasolabial folds is characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Systemic lupus erythematosus (malar/butterfly rash — photosensitive, acute cutaneous lupus, present in ~50% of SLE patients)
questions.explanation: Core concept: SLE malar rash = butterfly distribution, spares nasolabial folds, photosensitive. Why correct: Acute cutaneous lupus: malar rash — erythema/edema over cheeks and nose bridge, spares nasolabial folds (unlike rosacea). Photosensitive. ACR/EULAR criteria for SLE. Why A wrong: Rosacea — DOES NOT spare nasolabial folds, has papules/pustules, telangiectasia, no joint/systemic symptoms. Why C wrong: Dermatomyositis — heliotrope rash (periorbital), Gottron's papules (over MCP/IP joints), NOT malar butterfly. Why D wrong: Seborrheic dermatitis — INVOLVES nasolabial folds (greasy scales). Clinical relevance: Discoid lupus (chronic cutaneous): annular scarring plaques with follicular plugging, can lead to scarring alopecia. Subacute cutaneous lupus: annular polycyclic or papulosquamous, anti-Ro positive. Exam trap: SLE SPARES nasolabial folds; rosacea and seborrheic dermatitis INVOLVE them. This is a classic differentiator. Memory aid: 'Butterfly SLE = Spares nasoLabial (nasoLabial = Lupus-free zone).' Reference: IADVL Textbook – CTD.
